text: Fat and carbohydrate are oxidized in reciprocal proportions during aerobic exercise, with the balance dependent on substrate availability, exercise intensity, and exercise duration.
Fat metabolism is downregulated when carbohydrate availability increases, meaning high carbohydrate intake before or during exercise shifts fuel use away from fat oxidation.
Moving from moderate to intense aerobic exercise downregulates fat oxidation, shifting fuel utilization toward greater carbohydrate dependence.

Fat transport into muscle cells is regulated at multiple sites: plasma membrane transport, cytoplasmic binding and transport, intramuscular triacylglycerol synthesis/breakdown, and mitochondrial membrane transport.
Fat transport proteins translocate to plasma and mitochondrial membranes during exercise, increasing the capacity for fat oxidation in an intensity-dependent manner.
Adipose triglyceride lipase and hormone-sensitive lipase play distinct regulatory roles in skeletal muscle lipolysis during exercise, controlling the breakdown of intramuscular fat stores.
